student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Milestone Review -- Consolidating What You Know
lesson.menu_desc: Metade do caminho. Hoje nada e novo e tudo esta em jogo ao mesmo tempo: as estruturas que voce ja tem (third conditional, causative, cleft, inversion, future perfect) numa unica call de debrief com uma fornecedora francesa na defensiva -- mais o passo novo, o MIXED CONDITIONAL, que liga o erro do passado ao problema de hoje. E a diferenca entre RECONHECER o ingles e USAR o ingles -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- Milestone Review
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 11 -- Milestone Review

listenings.text: João, it's Marina, before your debrief call with Camille this afternoon, let me give you the state of play, because the numbers are not comfortable. We are at the halfway milestone, and on paper we are eight weeks behind on the signaling package. Here is what the record actually shows, and I want you to have it in front of you. The inspection protocol was signed by both sides in February. Camille's team then ran the factory acceptance test without three of the checks that protocol requires, and they logged it as a pass. If they had followed the protocol we agreed, we would not be arguing about compliance now, and the handover would still be on the original date. That is the leverage you have, and it is in writing, so use it calmly. Now, the elephant in the room. Part of this is ours. Our procurement team was late releasing the cable schedule, and if we had released it in March, some of their delay would have looked very different. Name that yourself before Camille does, because if she names it first, we lose the moral high ground. What I need from you by the end of the call is not blame. I need a corrective action plan, a weekly status update we can both see, and a realistic date for provisional acceptance that factors in the retesting. Escalate to me only if she refuses to retest the three failed checks. Everything else, you can close in that room. You have handled harder calls than this one. Good luck.

Can I tell you the one thing I wish somebody had told me at the halfway point of a career like yours? There is a gap, and almost nobody talks about it, between the English you RECOGNIZE and the English you USE. You have spent months now collecting structures. The third conditional, the causative, the cleft sentence, the inversion, the future perfect. When you read them, or when you hear a native speaker use them, you understand every one. That is recognition, and it is the easy half. The hard half is this: under pressure, in a room, with somebody defensive on the other side of the table, do those structures come OUT of your mouth, in the right order, without you reaching for them? That is use, and it is a completely different muscle. Here is the test I give people. Take one real fact from your project. Say it five different ways. If the supplier had followed the protocol, we would not be renegotiating the date. It was the missing checks that caused the delay. Never have we seen a factory test logged so carelessly. We are going to have the three units retested at their cost. By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan. Same fact, five structures, and every one of them was already in your head. The milestone review is not about learning something new. It is about turning everything you recognize into something you reach for without thinking. Do that, and the second half of the program is where your English stops being a subject you study and starts being a tool you forget you are holding.

patterns.title: The structures you already own -- and the one new bridge between them
cards: If they had followed the protocol, we wouldn't be behind now

if + past perfect, would + BASE verb. A PAST cause with a result you feel in the PRESENT. This is the new step today: it takes the third conditional you learned in Lesson 2 and lands the result in the room you are in now. "If Camille had escalated the shortage in March, the handover would be on schedule today."
If we weren't so exposed on compliance, I would have accepted it

if + past simple, would have + participle. A PRESENT, permanent truth that explains a PAST decision. "If I were the kind of engineer who let scope creep slide, I would have signed that off months ago." The condition is who you ARE; the result is what you DID.
It was the missing checks that caused the delay

It was ... that / What we need is ... Puts one fact under a spotlight and pushes the blame or the request to the exact right place. "What I need from your side is a corrective action plan, not an apology."
Never have we seen a test logged so carelessly
Negative adverbial + auxiliary before subject. Formal, emphatic, and it makes a supplier sit up. "Not only did the checks get skipped, but the result was logged as a pass."
We are going to have the three units retested at their cost

have + object + past participle. You arrange it; someone else does it; they pay. The language of a project engineer who directs work without doing it himself. "We will have the inspection protocol re-run before any sign-off."
By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan
will have + past participle. The tense of the deadline -- done BEFORE a date, and the one a contract can hold you to. "By the next milestone, every outstanding item will have been closed."

patterns.text: The mixed conditional is the ONLY new grammar today. Everything else on this card you have already met. The whole point of a milestone review is not to add a seventh structure -- it is to make these six come out of your mouth in a live call, in the right order, without you reaching for them. Recognition is the easy half. Use is the muscle we train today.

items.situation: Camille says the delay is entirely on Motiva's side. State the real cause, with the spotlight on the missing checks. (Cleft)
tips: It was the three skipped checks that caused the delay, not our cable schedule.
What the record shows is a factory acceptance test logged as a pass without the checks the protocol requires.

items.situation: Connect the past mistake to today's problem, in one sentence. (Mixed conditional)
tips: If your team had followed the inspection protocol, we wouldn't be renegotiating the milestone now.
If those checks had been run in February, the handover would still be on its original date today.

items.situation: Name the elephant in the room before she does: part of the delay is yours. (Honest, then leverage)
tips: Let me name the elephant in the room -- our procurement team released the cable schedule late, and I own that.
That said, a late schedule does not explain a factory test logged without three required checks.

items.situation: Direct the correction without doing it yourself, at her cost. (Causative)
tips: We are going to have the three failed units retested against the full protocol, at your cost.
We will have the results independently verified before any sign-off on this package.

items.situation: Make one formal, emphatic point that makes her sit up. (Inversion)
tips: Never have we accepted a factory acceptance test that skipped a mandatory hold point.
Not only were the checks skipped, but the result was recorded as a pass.

items.situation: Commit to a corrective plan with a date she can hold you to. (Future perfect + status update)
tips: By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan, and you will have a written status update every Friday.
By the next milestone, every outstanding item on this package will have been closed and signed off.

bank.label: Naming the real cause (cleft, from Lessons 5 & 8)
items: It was the three missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule.
What the signed protocol requires is a full set of checks before any pass is logged.
What I need from your side is a corrective action plan, not an explanation.

bank.label: Linking past to present (the new mixed conditional)
items: If your team had followed the protocol, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now.
If those checks had been run in February, the handover would still be on schedule today.
If I were an engineer who let scope creep slide, I would have signed this off months ago.

bank.label: Directing the fix (causative + inversion, from Lessons 6, 4 & 7)
items: We are going to have the three failed units retested against the full protocol, at your cost.
Never have we accepted a factory acceptance test that skipped a mandatory hold point.
Not only were the checks skipped, but the result was logged as a pass.

bank.label: Closing with a dated commitment (future perfect, from Lesson 9)
items: By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan and a realistic acceptance date.
You will have a written status update from me every Friday until this package is closed.
By the next milestone, every outstanding item will have been closed and signed off.
